sections.label: Section 488
sections.original: πῶς οὖν δυνήσει μανθάνειν ;
sections.translation: How , then , will you be able to learn ?

sections.label: Section 488b
sections.original: ἀμέλει καλῶς .
sections.translation: Excellently , of course .

sections.label: Section 489
sections.original: ἄγε νυν ὅπως , ὅταν τι προβάλλω σοι σοφὸν
sections.translation: Come , then , take care that , whenever I propound any clever dogma about abstruse matters , you catch it up immediately .

sections.label: Section 491
sections.original: τί δαί ; κυνηδὸν τὴν σοφίαν σιτήσομαι ;
sections.translation: What then ? Am I to feed upon wisdom like a dog ?

sections.label: Section 492
sections.original: ἄνθρωπος ἀμαθὴς οὑτοσὶ καὶ βάρβαρος .
sections.translation: This man is ignorant and brutish--I fear , old man , lest you will need blows . Come , let me see ; what do you do if any one beat you ?

sections.label: Section 494b
sections.original: τύπτομαι ,
sections.translation: I take the beating ; and then , when I have waited a little while , I call witnesses to prove it ; then again , after a short interval , I go to law .

sections.label: Section 497
sections.original: ἴθι νυν κατάθου θοἰμάτιον .
sections.translation: Come , then , lay down your cloak .

sections.label: Section 497b
sections.original: ἠδίκηκά τι ;
sections.translation: Have I done any wrong ?

sections.label: Section 498
sections.original: οὔκ , ἀλλὰ γυμνοὺς εἰσιέναι νομίζεται .
sections.translation: No ; but it is the rule to enter naked .

sections.label: Section 499
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὐχὶ φωράσων ἔγωγʼ εἰσέρχομαι .
sections.translation: But I do not enter to search for stolen goods .

sections.label: Section 500
sections.original: κατάθου . τί ληρεῖς ;
sections.translation: Lay it down . Why do you talk nonsense ?

sections.label: Section 500b
sections.original: εἰπὲ δή νύν μοι ·
sections.translation: [*] Now tell me this , pray .

sections.label: Section 501
sections.original: ἢν ἐπιμελὴς ὦ καὶ προθύμως μανθάνω ,
sections.translation: If I be diligent and learn zealously , to which of your disciples shall I become like ?

sections.label: Section 503
sections.original: οὐδὲν διοίσεις Χαιρεφῶντος τὴν φύσιν .
sections.translation: You will no way differ from Chaerephon in intellect .

sections.label: Section 504
sections.original: οἴμοι κακοδαίμων ἡμιθνὴς γενήσομαι .
sections.translation: Ah me , unhappy ! I shall become half-dead .

sections.label: Section 505
sections.original: οὐ μὴ λαλήσεις , ἀλλʼ ἀκολουθήσεις ἐμοὶ
sections.translation: Don ’ t chatter ; but quickly follow me hither with smartness .

sections.label: Section 506b
sections.original: ἐς τὼ χεῖρέ νυν
sections.translation: Then give me first into my hands a honeyed cake ; for I am afraid of descending within , as if into the cave of Trophonius .

sections.label: Section 509
sections.original: χώρει · τί κυπτάζεις ἔχων περὶ τὴν θύραν ;
sections.translation: Proceed ; why do you keep poking about the door ?

sections.label: Section 510
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἴθι χαίρων τῆς ἀνδρείας
sections.translation: Well , go in peace , for the sake of this your valour . May prosperity attend the man , because , being advanced into the vale of years ,

sections.label: Section 515
sections.original: νεωτέροις τὴν φύσιν αὑτοῦ
sections.translation: he imbues his intellect with modern subjects , and cultivates wisdom !

sections.label: Section 518
sections.original: ὦ θεώμενοι κατερῶ πρὸς ὑμᾶς ἐλευθέρως
sections.translation: Spectators , I will freely declare to you the truth , by Bacchus , who nurtured me !

sections.label: Section 520
sections.original: οὕτω νικήσαιμί τʼ ἐγὼ καὶ νομιζοίμην σοφός ,
sections.translation: So may I conquer , and be accounted skillful , as that , deeming you to be clever spectators , and this to be the cleverest of my comedies , I thought proper to let you first taste that comedy , which gave me the greatest labour . And then I retired from the contest defeated by vulgar fellows ,

sections.label: Section 525
sections.original: ἡττηθεὶς οὐκ ἄξιος ὤν · ταῦτʼ οὖν ὑμῖν μέμφομαι
sections.translation: though I did not deserve it . These things , therefore , I object to you , a learned audience , for whose sake I was expending this labour . But not even thus will I ever willingly desert the discerning portion of you . For since what time my Modest Man and my Rake were very highly praised here by an audience , with whom it is a pleasure even to hold converse , and I ( for I was still a virgin , and it was not lawful for me as yet to have children )

sections.label: Section 530
sections.original: κἀγώ , παρθένος γὰρ ἔτʼ ἦν , κοὐκ ἐξῆν πώ μοι τεκεῖν ,
sections.translation: exposed my offspring , and another girl took it up , and owned it , and you generously reared and educated it , from this time I have had sure pledges of your good will toward me . Now , therefore , like that well-known Electra , has this comedy come seeking ,

sections.label: Section 535
sections.original: ζητοῦσʼ ἦλθʼ , ἤν που ʼπιτύχῃ θεαταῖς οὕτω σοφοῖς ·
sections.translation: if haply it meet with an audience so clever , for it will recognize , if it should see , the lock of its brother . But see how modest she is by nature , who , in the first place , has come , having stitched to her no leathern phallus hanging down , red at the top , and thick , to set the boys a laughing ;

sections.label: Section 540
sections.original: οὐδʼ ἔσκωψε τοὺς φαλακρούς , οὐδὲ κόρδαχʼ εἵλκυσεν ,
sections.translation: nor yet jeered the bald-headed , nor danced the cordax ; nor does the old man who speaks the verses beat the person near him with his staff , keeping out of sight wretched ribaldry ; nor has she rushed in with torches , nor does she shout iou , iou ; but has come relying on herself and her verses .

sections.label: Section 545
sections.original: κἀγὼ μὲν τοιοῦτος ἀνὴρ ὢν ποιητὴς οὐ κομῶ ,
sections.translation: And I , although so excellent a poet , do not give myself airs , nor do I seek to deceive you by twice and thrice bringing forward the same pieces ; but I am always clever at introducing new fashions , not at all resembling each other , and all of them clever ; who struck Cleon in the belly when at the height of his power ,

sections.label: Section 550
sections.original: κοὐκ ἐτόλμησʼ αὖθις ἐπεμπηδῆσʼ αὐτῷ κειμένῳ .
sections.translation: and could not bear to attack him afterward when he was down . But these scribblers , when once Hyperbolus has given them a handle , keep ever trampling on this wretched man and his mother . Eupolis , indeed , first of all craftily introduced his Maricas , having basely , base fellow , spoiled by altering my play of the Knights ,

sections.label: Section 555
sections.original: προσθεὶς αὐτῷ γραῦν μεθύσην τοῦ κόρδακος οὕνεχʼ , ἣν
sections.translation: having added to it , for the sake of the cordax , a drunken old woman , whom Phrynichus long ago poetized , whom the whale was for devouring . Then again Hermippus made verses on Hyperbolus ; and now all others press hard upon Hyperbolus , imitating my simile of the eels .

sections.label: Section 560
sections.original: ὅστις οὖν τούτοισι γελᾷ , τοῖς ἐμοῖς μὴ χαιρέτω ·
sections.translation: Whoever , therefore , laughs at these , let him not take pleasure in my attempts ; but if you are delighted with me and my inventions , in times to come you will seem to be wise .

sections.label: Section 563
sections.original: ὑψιμέδοντα μὲν θεῶν
sections.translation: I first invoke , to join our choral band , the mighty Jupiter , ruling on high ,

sections.label: Section 565
sections.original: πρῶτα μέγαν κικλήσκω ·
sections.translation: the monarch of gods ; and the potent master of the trident , the fierce upheaver of earth and briny sea ; and our father of great renown ,

sections.label: Section 570
sections.original: Αἰθέρα σεμνότατον βιοθρέμμονα πάντων ·
sections.translation: most august Aether , life-supporter of all ; and the horse-guider , who fills the plain of the earth with exceeding bright beams , a mighty deity among gods and mortals .

sections.label: Section 575
sections.original: ὦ σοφώτατοι θεαταὶ δεῦρο τὸν νοῦν προσέχετε .
sections.translation: Most clever spectators , come , give us your attention ; for having been injured , we blame you to your faces . For though we benefit the state most of all the gods , to us alone of the deities you do not offer sacrifice nor yet pour libations , who watch over you . For if there should be any expedition

sections.label: Section 580
sections.original: μηδενὶ ξὺν νῷ , τότʼ ἢ βροντῶμεν ἢ ψακάζομεν .
sections.translation: without prudence , then we either thunder or drizzle small rain . And then , when you were for choosing as your general the Paphlagonian tanner , hateful to the gods , we contracted our brows and were enraged ; and thunder burst through the lightning ; and the Moon forsook her usual paths ; and the Sun

sections.label: Section 585
sections.original: τὴν θρυαλλίδʼ εἰς ἑαυτὸν εὐθέως ξυνελκύσας
sections.translation: immediately drew in his wick to himself , and declared he would not give you light , if Cleon should be your general . Nevertheless you chose him . For they say that ill counsel is in this city ; that the gods , however , turn all these your mismanagements to a prosperous issue .

sections.label: Section 590
sections.original: ὡς δὲ καὶ τοῦτο ξυνοίσει ῥᾳδίως διδάξομεν ·
sections.translation: And how this also shall be advantageous , we will easily teach you . If you should convict the cormorant Cleon of bribery and embezzlement , and then make fast his neck in the stocks , the affair will turn out for the state to the ancient form again , if you have mismanaged in any way , and to a prosperous issue .

sections.label: Section 595
sections.original: ἀμφί μοι αὖτε Φοῖβʼ ἄναξ
sections.translation: Hear me again , King Phoebus , Delian Apollo , who inhabitest the high-peaked Cynthian rock ! And thou , blessed goddess , who inhabitest the all-golden house of Ephesus ,

sections.label: Section 600
sections.original: οἶκον ἐν ᾧ κόραι σε Λυδῶν μεγάλως σέβουσιν ,
sections.translation: in which Lydian damsels greatly reverence thee ; and thou , our national goddess , swayer of the aegis , Minerva , guardian of the city ! And thou , reveler Bacchus , who , inhabiting the Parnassian rock , sparklest with torches ,

sections.label: Section 605
sections.original: Βάκχαις Δελφίσιν ἐμπρέπων ,
sections.translation: conspicuous among the Delphic Bacchanals !

sections.label: Section 607
sections.original: ἡνίχʼ ἡμεῖς δεῦρʼ ἀφορμᾶσθαι παρεσκευάσμεθα ,
sections.translation: When we had got ready to set out hither , the Moon met us , and commanded us first to greet the Athenians and their allies ;

sections.label: Section 610
sections.original: εἶτα θυμαίνειν ἔφασκε · δεινὰ γὰρ πεπονθέναι
sections.translation: and then declared that she was angry , for that she had suffered dreadful things , though she benefits you all , not in words , but openly . In the first place , not less than a drachma every month for torches ; so that also all , when they went out of an evening , were wont to say , Boy , don ’ t buy a torch , for the moonlight is beautiful .

sections.label: Section 615
sections.original: ἄλλα τʼ εὖ δρᾶν φησιν , ὑμᾶς δʼ οὐκ ἄγειν τὰς ἡμέρας
sections.translation: And she says she confers other benefits on you , but that you do not observe the days at all correctly , but confuse them up and down ; so that she says the gods are constantly threatening her , when they are defrauded of their dinner , and depart home , not having met with the regular feast according to the number of the days .

sections.label: Section 620
sections.original: κᾆθʼ ὅταν θύειν δέῃ , στρεβλοῦτε καὶ δικάζετε ·
sections.translation: And then , when you ought to be sacrificing , you are inflicting tortures and litigating . And often , while we gods are observing a fast , when we mourn for Memnon or Sarpedon , you are pouring libations and laughing . For which reason Hyperbolus , having obtained the lot this year to be Hieromnemon , was afterward deprived by us gods

sections.label: Section 625
sections.original: τὸν στέφανον ἀφῃρέθη · μᾶλλον γὰρ οὕτως εἴσεται
sections.translation: of his crown ; for thus he will know better that he ought to spend the days of his life according to the Moon .

sections.label: Section 627
sections.original: μὰ τὴν Ἀναπνοὴν μὰ τὸ Χάος μὰ τὸν Ἀέρα
sections.translation: By Respiration , and Chaos , and Air , I have not seen any man so boorish , nor so impracticable , nor so stupid , nor so forgetful ;

sections.label: Section 630
sections.original: ὅστις σκαλαθυρμάτιʼ ἄττα μικρὰ μανθάνων
sections.translation: who , while learning some little petty quibbles , forgets them before he has learned them . Nevertheless I will certainly call him out here to the light . Where is Strepsiades ? Come forth with your couch .

sections.label: Section 634
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὐκ ἐῶσί μʼ ἐξενεγκεῖν οἱ κόρεις .
sections.translation: ( from within ). The bugs do not permit me to bring it forth .

sections.label: Section 635
sections.original: ἀνύσας τι κατάθου καὶ πρόσεχε τὸν νοῦν .
sections.translation: Make haste and lay it down ; and give me your attention .

sections.label: Section 635b
sections.original: ἰδού .
sections.translation: Very well .

sections.label: Section 636
sections.original: ἄγε δὴ τί βούλει πρῶτα νυνὶ μανθάνειν
sections.translation: Come now ; what do you now wish to learn first of those things in none of which you have ever been instructed ? Tell me . About measures , or rhythms , or verses ?

sections.label: Section 639
sections.original: περὶ τῶν μέτρων ἔγωγʼ · ἔναγχος γάρ ποτε
sections.translation: I should prefer to learn about measures ; for it is but lately I was cheated out of two choenices by a meal-huckster .

sections.label: Section 641
sections.original: οὐ τοῦτʼ ἐρωτῶ σʼ , ἀλλʼ ὅ τι κάλλιστον μέτρον
sections.translation: I do not ask you this , but which you account the most beautiful measure ; the trimetre or the tetrameter ?

sections.label: Section 643
sections.original: ἐγὼ μὲν οὐδὲν πρότερον ἡμιεκτέου .
sections.translation: I think nothing superior to the semisextarius .

sections.label: Section 644
sections.original: οὐδὲν λέγεις ὦνθρωπε .
sections.translation: You talk nonsense , man .

sections.label: Section 644b
sections.original: περίδου νυν ἐμοί ,
sections.translation: Make a wager then with me , if the semisextarius be not a tetrameter .

sections.label: Section 646
sections.original: ἐς κόρακας , ὡς ἄγροικος εἶ καὶ δυσμαθής .
sections.translation: Go to the devil ! How boorish you are and dull of learning . Perhaps you may be able to learn about rhythms .

sections.label: Section 648
sections.original: τί δέ μʼ ὠφελήσουσʼ οἱ ῥυθμοὶ πρὸς τἄλφιτα ;
sections.translation: But what good will rhythms do me for a living ?

sections.label: Section 649
sections.original: πρῶτον μὲν εἶναι κομψὸν ἐν συνουσίᾳ ,
sections.translation: In the first place , to be clever at an entertainment , understanding what rhythm is for the war-dance , and what , again , according to the dactyle .

sections.label: Section 652
sections.original: κατὰ δάκτυλον ; νὴ τὸν Δίʼ , ἀλλʼ οἶδʼ .
sections.translation: According to the dactyle ? By Jove , but I know it !
